description: What SOC operations interviews test: alert triage and investigation speed, telemetry reading, escalation judgment, tool proficiency, shift handoff quality, and process maturity. Also tests resilience under ambiguity - real SOC work rarely comes with clean scenarios.

junior.label: Junior / Associate (Tier 1)
characteristics: Follows pre-written runbooks step-by-step without understanding the underlying reasoning
Can acknowledge an alert but struggles to triage severity without explicit guidance
Describes alert review as 'checking if it's a true positive or false positive' with no articulated decision framework
Knows SIEM exists and can navigate dashboards but cannot write queries from scratch
Cannot explain what a Tier 1 vs Tier 2 analyst should handle differently
Has a binary view of alerts: malicious or benign, with no nuance for suspicious-but-unconfirmed
Does not pivot from an initial alert to related events without being told to
Describes SLA as 'acknowledge within X minutes' without understanding why SLAs exist
Cannot explain what an analyst should include in an escalation note for Tier 2
Knows common attacks (phishing, malware) but cannot map them to specific observable indicators
Treats every alert as equally urgent until told otherwise
Cannot explain what 'false positive rate' means for their team's alert queue health
Has no framework for documenting investigation findings in a case management tool
Cannot explain the difference between a detection, an alert, and an incident
Does not know what questions to ask a reporting user during a phishing investigation
example_phrases: I would look at the alert and follow the runbook
I'd check VirusTotal to see if the hash is known bad
I'd escalate it to my senior analyst if I'm not sure
I usually handle about 30-40 alerts per shift
I'd block the IP at the firewall if it's malicious
I close it as a false positive if it looks legitimate
I'd notify the user's manager if there was suspicious activity on their account
red_flags: Claims Tier 2 experience but cannot explain how they would escalate with context
Says 'I've worked in a SOC for 2 years' but cannot describe their alert workflow with any specifics
Treats all phishing emails the same regardless of targeting or payload
Cannot distinguish between a successful login from an unusual location and an actual compromise
Thinks closing an alert as false positive requires no documentation
Cannot explain what information a Tier 2 analyst needs from a Tier 1 escalation
expected_tools: SIEM dashboards (reading, not writing queries)
VirusTotal / urlscan.io / AbuseIPDB
EDR console (basic alert review)
Ticketing system (ServiceNow, Jira, TheHive)
Email header analyzer

mid.label: Mid-Level (Tier 2)
characteristics: Builds their own investigation workflow from an initial alert without prompting
Writes effective SIEM queries: filters by host, user, time range, specific Event IDs
Triages with context: considers asset criticality, user role, and business context in severity determination
Understands alert fatigue and can identify when a rule is generating too many false positives
Correlates multiple alerts into a single incident narrative
Pivots from an endpoint alert to network traffic to identity logs to construct a complete picture
Can analyze a phishing email: headers, URL defanging, attachment type, macro inspection
Knows logon type codes: Type 2 (interactive), Type 3 (network), Type 10 (RemoteInteractive), Type 7 (unlock)
Can identify suspicious scheduled tasks from Event ID 4698 with parent process context
Writes a clear escalation note with: what happened, what evidence was gathered, what was ruled out, recommended next step
Understands when to contain vs. when to monitor (active vs. dormant threat)
Can distinguish between a compromised account and a misconfigured one based on behavior patterns
Knows basic network indicators: unusual external DNS queries, anomalous data volumes, beaconing to new domains
Has experience with alert triage at scale and developed personal heuristics for prioritization
Can identify when multiple low-severity alerts form a high-severity pattern
example_phrases: I start by looking at the user account involved - is this a service account, a privileged user, or a standard employee? That changes the risk profile completely

The alert fired on 4624 type 3 logon, but when I pulled the surrounding context I found a 4648 thirty seconds earlier from the same source IP - that's explicit credential use, which elevated the severity
I never close a phishing ticket without pulling the email headers to check for spoofed 'From' vs 'Reply-To' and confirming whether the URL resolves to a credential harvesting page
The rule was firing 200 times a day. I queried the SIEM and found 95% of hits were from our update servers running certutil legitimately. I updated the rule to exclude that process tree
When I escalate to Tier 2 or IR, I include: timeline of events, artifacts I collected, accounts involved, systems impacted, and what I've already ruled out - so they don't duplicate my work
A single failed login is noise. Twenty failed logins followed by one success from the same IP is an incident. I correlate both before making a call
red_flags: Cannot write a KQL or SPL query to pull events for a specific user over a time range
Escalates everything to Tier 2 without a clear rationale
Cannot explain the difference between logon type 3 and logon type 10
Thinks phishing analysis ends at 'check VirusTotal for the URL'
Has never identified a multi-stage attack from correlated low-severity alerts
Cannot explain what constitutes an effective escalation note
expected_tools: Splunk (SPL queries with stats, eval, lookup)
Microsoft Sentinel (KQL with let, join, summarize)
CrowdStrike / SentinelOne / Defender for Endpoint (investigation mode)
Proofpoint / Microsoft Defender for O365 (email analysis)
TheHive / XSOAR / ServiceNow (case management)
Shodan / Censys (external context for IPs)

senior.label: Senior (Tier 2/3 or SOC Lead)
characteristics: Builds and maintains SOC playbooks that reflect real investigation workflows, not just compliance templates
Mentors Tier 1 analysts and can explain why each step in a runbook exists
Identifies systematic alert fatigue problems and proposes rule tuning or data quality improvements
Can build a SIEM dashboard or detection rule without help from a detection engineer
Performs hypothesis-driven threat hunting to find threats that haven't triggered any alerts
Has experience with cloud alert triage: Azure AD Conditional Access failures, AWS GuardDuty findings, GCP Security Command Center
Can correlate identity, network, and endpoint telemetry to build a complete attacker timeline
Understands SOC metrics and can critique them: MTTA, MTTR, alert volume trends, analyst capacity
Has experience with insider threat investigations: user behavior analytics, DLP alerts, HR correlation
Can identify when an incident requires external notification (legal, HR, executive, regulatory)
Conducts shift handoffs that include active investigations, contextual notes, and recommended follow-up
Has experience with tuning detection rules to improve signal-to-noise ratio at scale
Can design triage criteria for a new alert type when no runbook exists yet
Understands SOAR and can evaluate which playbook steps are safe to automate
Has led post-incident reviews and turns findings into documented process improvements
example_phrases: The playbook said to isolate the host, but the host was a production database. I stopped and looped in the incident commander before acting - automatic containment would have been worse than the attack
I built a threat hunting query in Sentinel that looked for hosts making DNS requests to newly registered domains - caught a loader that had bypassed our EDR's initial detection

When I review my team's escalations, I'm checking for three things: did they pivot to correlated events, did they rule out benign explanations, and did they include enough context for Tier 2 to act without re-doing the work

The MTTA looked fine on paper - 8 minutes average. But when I broke it down by alert type, I found we were fast on easy phishing but averaging 45 minutes on credential abuse, which is the thing that matters most
I designed our cloud triage runbook after noticing we had no documented procedure for GuardDuty findings - wrote it based on six months of investigation patterns the team had built up informally
red_flags: Cannot explain what threat hunting is different from alert triage
Has never written a playbook from scratch, only followed them
Cannot explain what SOAR is or name a specific automation they've built
Does not understand SOC metrics or how to critique them
Has no experience with cloud environments in the SOC context
expected_tools: Advanced SIEM (Splunk ES correlation searches, Sentinel Analytics rules with ML)
SOAR platforms (Palo Alto XSOAR, Splunk SOAR, Sentinel Automation)
Threat hunting platforms (CrowdStrike Overwatch, Defender Hunting, Chronicle)
UEBA tools (Microsoft Sentinel UEBA, Exabeam, LogRhythm)
Cloud-native alerting: GuardDuty, Defender for Cloud, GCP SCC
expected_frameworks: MITRE ATT&CK (triage enrichment and hunting hypotheses)

staff.label: Staff / SOC Manager / Principal
characteristics: Designs the SOC operating model: staffing ratios, tier structure, escalation matrix, on-call protocol
Defines and tracks SOC KPIs tied to business risk: reduction in MTTD, analyst handling capacity, detection coverage by threat actor
Has built or managed the SOC tooling stack: SIEM selection, EDR deployment, SOAR implementation
Drives SOC automation strategy: defines what to automate, when human judgment is irreplaceable, and manages automation debt
Manages vendor relationships and SLAs for MDR, MSSP, or threat intelligence providers
Designs analyst career development paths: Tier 1 to Tier 2 promotion criteria, training programs, certifications
Has operated through major incidents and can reflect on organizational decisions made under pressure
Understands regulatory audit requirements for SOC logs and can prepare for a PCI DSS or SOC 2 audit
Measures and improves analyst mental health and burnout risk alongside performance metrics
Connects SOC operations to board-level risk conversations: translates analyst workload into business exposure
Evaluates and onboards new threat intelligence feeds with a clear ROI framework
Has designed 24x7 SOC coverage models and solved the shift structure problem
Can critique SIEM architecture and recommend scalability improvements
Designs tabletop exercises specifically to stress-test SOC response procedures
example_phrases: Our SOAR automation handles the top 15 alert types automatically - that's 60% of our alert volume without analyst touch. The humans focus on the ambiguous cases that need judgment

I track analyst 'decisional accuracy' as a metric - what percentage of their escalations turned into confirmed incidents vs. what percentage were unnecessary escalations. It tells me more than alert throughput
We moved from a 3-tier SOC to a 2-tier model with dedicated detection engineers and found analyst satisfaction went up and MTTD went down - the tier 3 bottleneck was killing us
When I evaluate an MSSP, I ask for their detection engineering roadmap and their mean time between detection rule updates. Most can't answer the second question, which tells you a lot
SOC burnout is a real risk. I monitor analyst alert handling velocity over time - when someone's speed drops 30%, that's a signal, not a performance problem
red_flags: Has no SOC metrics beyond alert volume and ticket close rate
Cannot explain the tradeoffs of different SOC staffing models
Has never evaluated a SIEM or SOAR vendor
Cannot speak to analyst career development
Does not consider analyst wellbeing as part of SOC operations
expected_tools: Enterprise SIEM (Splunk ES, Sentinel, Chronicle, Elastic) at admin/architect level
SOAR platforms (XSOAR, Splunk SOAR) at design level
Threat intelligence platforms (Recorded Future, Anomali)
Workforce management tools for 24x7 shift coverage
GRC platforms (for compliance reporting from SOC data)
expected_frameworks: SOC visibility triad at program level
MITRE ATT&CK as program coverage measurement tool
Regulatory compliance frameworks (PCI DSS, HIPAA, SOX) as they apply to SOC logging requirements

positive: Describes triage as a structured decision process, not a gut check
Pivots naturally between data sources (endpoint -> network -> identity) without prompting
Mentions documenting findings even for false positives
Considers asset criticality and business context in severity assessment
Distinguishes between what they know, what they suspect, and what requires more investigation
References specific queries or commands they would run, not just tool names
Thinks about attacker objectives and what else they might be doing beyond the initial alert
Has opinions on how to improve processes they've worked in
negative: Cannot construct a coherent pivot chain from an initial alert
Cannot write a basic SIEM query for their primary tool
Uses 'suspicious' without being able to articulate what specifically is suspicious
Treats alert triage and threat hunting as the same activity
Cannot explain what information they would include in an escalation note
Has no concept of false positive rate or how it affects team health
Cannot explain a SOC metric beyond 'number of tickets closed'
